answer.text: A financial instrument whose value is derived from the value of an underlying asset
A financial instrument that always trades on stock exchanges
A type of equity share issued by a company to raise capital
A government bond with a fixed maturity period

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A derivative is a financial instrument whose value is derived from or depends on the value of an underlying asset. The underlying can be stocks, bonds, commodities, currencies, interest rates, or market indices. **The Trap:** "Always trades on stock exchanges" is wrong because derivatives can also trade OTC (over-the-counter). Equity shares and bonds are primary securities, not derivatives. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series I (Currency Derivatives) and NISM Series IV (Interest Rate Derivatives) also test this fundamental definition. **Key Takeaway:** The defining feature of a derivative is that its value is DERIVED from an underlying asset — it has no independent value of its own.
